# Evaluation Report

This evaluation assesses whether the AircraftVerse Design Explorer meets the objectives and success criteria defined in the [Project Charta](project_charta.qmd).

## Evaluation Objectives

The evaluation focuses on three dimensions:

- **Analytical value**: users can identify viable designs, compare design families and inspect trade-offs.
- **Communicative value**: the dashboard explains the main patterns clearly for aviation students.
- **Usability**: users can complete typical tasks without needing to inspect raw data files.

## Evaluation Method

The prototype was evaluated with a lightweight task-based review and a heuristic review. This is appropriate for the project scope because the dashboard is a course visualization product and not a production engineering tool.

The task-based review uses representative tasks derived from the aviation-student persona.

: Evaluation tasks and expected outcomes. {#tbl-evaluation-tasks}

| Task | Expected evidence in dashboard | Success criterion |
|---|---|---|
| Identify how many designs are viable | Viability Landscape shows flight status counts and percentages | User can distinguish viable, hover-capable and non-viable designs |
| Find a strong hub family | Design Families and insight cards highlight high-performing hub families | User can name one family and support the answer with chart values |
| Compare range and hover trade-off | High-Performing Recipes shows the Pareto trade-off scatterplot | User can identify designs or recipes that balance both metrics |
| Explain a technical term | Glossary explains hub family, battery cells, hover-capable and Pareto front | User does not need to read raw AircraftVerse schema |

## Results

: Evaluation results by success criterion. {#tbl-evaluation-results}

| Criterion | Result | Assessment |
|---|---|---|
| Users identify at least three meaningful relationships | The dashboard surfaces viability differences, hub-family differences, fuselage/battery recipe differences and range-hover trade-offs | Met |
| Visualization is easy to use | Tabs separate analytical tasks; filters apply globally; glossary supports interpretation | Mostly met |
| Find a best or suitable design in less than two minutes | High-Performing Recipes and tables allow quick identification of strong recipes and individual designs | Met in prototype review |
| Smooth performance with 27,714 points | App uses a compact processed dataset and samples points in the free explorer | Met for local prototype |
| Reproducibility | Data preparation scripts and processed dataset are included | Met after final repository cleanup |

## Discussion

The most successful aspect of the prototype is that it does not start with individual aircraft records. Instead, it first shows viability and then moves toward design families and recipes. This matches the needs of aviation students, who usually need to understand patterns and trade-offs rather than inspect thousands of raw designs.

The dashboard also avoids relying only on maximum values. Median and 90th percentile summaries make the comparisons more stable and less dependent on single extreme designs.

Remaining limitations:

- The data is simulated and cannot prove physical aircraft performance.
- Evaluation was limited to internal review and task walkthroughs.
- Some AircraftVerse design concepts remain technical even after label simplification.
- The dashboard does not include predictive modelling or engineering validation, which are outside the project scope.

## Checkpoint Decision

The prototype meets the project objectives and is ready for deployment after final documentation, repository cleanup and publication of the Streamlit app.

The recommended finalization steps are:

- deploy the Streamlit dashboard
- render and publish the Quarto documentation
- verify that the processed dataset loads without local raw files
- use the dashboard screenshots as fallback material for the oral presentation
